text: During a left-sided lobectomy, the surgeon encounters bleeding and uses the Cartier Arm One to apply pressure with a sponge on the pulmonary artery and vein. The camera is repositioned through the assistant port to maintain visual contact with the bleeder. Instruments are removed to prepare for conversion to a posterolateral thoracotomy, ensuring a clear view of the bleeding site.

During a left lower lobectomy, the surgeon encounters a positive hilar node and performs a careful dissection. A spondylole is positioned above the dissection site to apply pressure in case of bleeding. As anticipated, bleeding occurs, and the surgeon uses the spondylole to hold pressure on the pulmonary artery, a low-pressure system. The camera is momentarily obscured by blood but is quickly cleaned to maintain visibility.

The surgeon uses a suction device to remove blood from the surgical field while maintaining pressure on the bleeder with the medial arm. The pulmonary artery is controlled with gentle pressure to prevent further tearing. The surgeon steps away from the console to ensure pressure is held for three minutes, allowing potential cessation of bleeding.

The surgeon holds pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm while preparing for a posterolateral thoracotomy. The camera is repositioned from arm two to the assistant port, maintaining visualization of the sponge stick on the bleeder. The lateral three robotic ports are removed to facilitate the thoracotomy.

After completing the thoracotomy, the surgeon manually controls the bleeding through the thoracotomy incision. Arm one of the robotic system is removed, and the entire robotic apparatus is repositioned to ensure a safe conversion.

The surgeon attempts an anteromedial basilar segmentectomy on the left lower lobe, working on small pulmonary artery branches in a confined space. A robotic stapler is used, but bleeding occurs, prompting the use of a sponge roll to hold pressure. The surgeon prepares for conversion to thoracotomy, coordinating with the anesthesiologist and surgical team to manage the bleeding and open thoracotomy supplies.

The surgeon maintains pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm (arm one) while the assistant removes the camera from arm two and places it through the assistant port for continuous visualization. The lateral robotic ports are removed, and the surgeon prepares for a posterolateral thoracotomy through the fifth intercostal space. The robotic arm holding pressure is slid out, and the robot is moved aside to gain manual control of the bleeder.
